author: @PhilR0gers
text:
Those cast iron Victorian columns you pointed out could do with stripping and repainting.  They look as if they have been over-painted (quite badly) so many times that the detail is becoming lost under some very uneven layers of paint.
I had the same issue with plaster corbels in my house.  The previous owners had over-painted them so many times that there was barely any detail left.  I managed to strip all the paint back, and they look fantastic now.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
On a philosophical level,  There's an old 1972 TV series/book called ways of seeing by John berger. Well worth a watch *and* a read. It's considered somewhat of a classic for degree level art courses. Amongst many topics does a great job of Discussing how mass reproduction  and location one views an image in alters how one perceives the art work. And thus also altered art itself.
3D scanners likely to have both similar and unexpected effects.

author: @mattmill30
text: 'scan the world' as a concept is great. But it's needs to be hosted by the community for the community. Centralised platforms are always exploited by the parent company once they have established a monopoly.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The problem is folks may say stuff like that - but then they never do much about it. If they do something it normally languishes on the fringe or just dies away. So, although 'for the community by the community' sounds awesome I have my doubts it would amount to much tbh - sorry

replies.author: @mattmill30
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I agree with your assessment of the current state of affairs. But you've highlighted a symptom of a broader philosophical/sociological problem which is the consequence of top-down societal governance and individuals becoming consumers of corporations and deferring ownership and responsibility to a third-party/authority.

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text:
Scan the world... what a fascinating idea that is! 

The armour expert, Tobias Capwell, needs to see this. His recent work on the information about English armour from effigies would benefit hugely from being able to study them in 3 dimensions, I would imagine. Many of these effigies are only just being photographically recorded, going even further will put important information in the hands of scholars the world over who are studying this subject. The same applies to medieval weaponry in various collections. They can be scanned and then printed by anyone who wishes to study the proportions of these artefacts and will enable those making replicas, such as the deal Matt Easton has with The Royal Armouries and Windlass Blades in India, to create super-accurate copies, as they will be able to measure any dimension they please of the original, when they need to. At the moment Matt takes photographs and measurements of a sword and then there is a process of iteration and prototyping. That process would be much more simple if Matt was able to send a hi-fidelity scan along with everything else. 

How great would it be if everything in the Wallace Collection was scanned in and shared?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely agree with you mate

author: @jerrywhidby.
text: That's a great idea. Especially in regions prone to war. Even if the original is lost a reproduction can be made.

author: @grendel1960a
text:
that sounds like a great place to upload 3d scans so they are not lost, i have scanned some wooden carvings from picnic benches on the walks around canterbury, so will be uploading those there soon.
Another use made for 3d printed objects is by a friend who does talks about astronomy to schools, scouts, guides and other organisations, he has downloaded and printed out section of the lunar surface supplied by nasa, and uses those as exhibits for the talks, recently this enabled a group of blind people to feel the surface of the moon and other astronomical objects he has printed. 3d printing has many uses, some which we probably have never thought of yet

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate and I am seriously looking forward to seeing your scans

author: @jeffbybee5207
text: When you scan something how long does it take and can you scan living things including animals? Do you take multiple pictures from different angles? Best wishes and loves the dog behind you when you were sitting

replies.author: @grendel1960a
replies.text: I have the creality scanner and the software keeps taking photos and aligning them to create a model- you slowly move the scanner across the object to be scanned and it builds the image, yes you can do people if they stand still, it then processes the scans and creates a mesh and from that an stl file you can print on your printer. the final scan can be made from several thousand images.

replies.author: @jeffbybee5207
replies.text: @grendel1960a thankyou that's really neat. I've loved 3d for decades having different camera and the last pentax stereo attachment sold in America  . Best wishes
